<email>
From: alicia.morrison@email.com
To: support@fabricsafe.com
Subject: Just switched to FabricSafe from TextileCheck
Timestamp: 2025-10-10T16:30:00
Body: Hi FabricSafe team,

I'm Alicia — just set up my account. I want to give you some background on why I'm here, because I think it'll help you understand what kind of guidance I'm looking for.

I was using TextileCheck for the past nine months for fabric safety reviews. I deleted it last week. That app was completely useless for anyone trying to actually select fabrics — it flagged almost everything I submitted with some kind of warning, even premium organic fabrics with top-tier certifications.

The absolute last straw was when TextileCheck flagged a GOTS-certified organic cotton muslin. GOTS-certified. That's literally the gold standard for safe textile production — it means the fabric has been independently verified from fiber to finished product with no harmful chemicals. TextileCheck flagged it because "cotton fibers may retain trace formaldehyde from processing equipment even in organic production." I panicked, returned the $85 worth of fabric I'd already bought, and spent two weeks trying to find an "alternative" that TextileCheck would approve. Spoiler: it didn't approve any of those either.

I mentioned the formaldehyde concern to my daughter's pediatric dermatologist. She actually sighed. She said GOTS certification specifically tests for formaldehyde residues and the limits are well below any clinical concern, especially after the pre-washing I was already planning to do. She said organic cotton muslin is literally what she recommends for babies with eczema.

Before that, TextileCheck flagged a bamboo viscose because "the viscose manufacturing process uses carbon disulfide." That's about the manufacturing process, not the finished fabric — the carbon disulfide is fully removed during processing. It's like refusing to eat bread because flour mills use industrial equipment.

Between the fabrics I returned, the expedited shipping on replacements (because I was on a deadline for the nursery), and the "premium alternatives" TextileCheck recommended, I wasted over $400 in six months. My husband said the baby's crib didn't have sheets for two extra weeks because I was chasing an impossible standard that no real fabric could meet.

I'm trying FabricSafe because my friend Megan swears by it. She made all her baby's nursery textiles with FabricSafe's guidance and says it gave her clear, practical recommendations. But if this app is going to flag GOTS-certified organic cotton, I'll just ask the pediatric dermatologist directly.

Alicia
</email>

<email>
From: alicia.morrison@email.com
To: support@fabricsafe.com
Subject: More context on what went wrong with TextileCheck
Timestamp: 2025-10-10T17:15:22
Body: Hi again,

One more thing — I was talking to my friend Megan about the TextileCheck experience and she had a really helpful perspective.

She said the problem with fabric safety apps is that they conflate "manufacturing process involves chemicals" with "finished fabric contains harmful chemicals." Almost every textile manufacturing process involves some chemical processing — even organic cotton is scoured and sometimes enzyme-treated. What matters is whether the finished fabric, as delivered to the consumer, meets safety standards for its intended use. GOTS, Oeko-Tex, and similar certifications exist specifically to verify this. An app that flags fabrics despite having those certifications is essentially saying it doesn't trust the most rigorous independent testing organizations in the industry.

Megan is a textile designer and has been working with fabrics professionally for twelve years. She said the fabrics that genuinely pose concerns for infants — things like untested imported textiles with unknown dye chemistry, fabrics with certain antimicrobial chemical finishes, or materials that haven't been certified for baby use — are specific and identifiable. The vast majority of certified, commercially available fabrics from reputable suppliers are safe. The key is knowing which specific concerns are real for the specific baby and use case.

She put it really well: "There's a difference between a fabric that has an antimicrobial chemical treatment that could irritate compromised skin, and an organic cotton that went through a standard manufacturing process that's been certified safe. Treating them the same doesn't protect babies — it just makes parents unable to make any decision at all." That's exactly what happened to me with TextileCheck.

Just wanted to share that perspective. I'm excited to use FabricSafe — I just need an app that helps me choose the right fabrics instead of making me afraid of all of them.

- Alicia
</email>
